callouts.label: Cell wall
callouts.hint: Thick tan/brown outer boundary of the prokaryotic cell on the left side.
callouts.description: A rigid outer layer found in most prokaryotes, composed of peptidoglycan in bacteria. The cell wall provides structural support, maintains cell shape, and protects against osmotic lysis. It lies outside the plasma membrane and is a key target for antibiotics like penicillin, which inhibit peptidoglycan synthesis.
callouts.ap_tip: Plant cells also have cell walls, but theirs are made of cellulose — not peptidoglycan. The AP exam tests this distinction. Gram-positive bacteria have thick peptidoglycan walls; Gram-negative have a thin wall plus an outer membrane. Antibiotics like penicillin target peptidoglycan synthesis.

callouts.label: Plasma membrane
callouts.hint: Thin blue inner boundary of the prokaryotic cell, just inside the cell wall.
callouts.description: A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that encloses the prokaryotic cell. It regulates the movement of substances into and out of the cell through selective permeability. In prokaryotes, the plasma membrane also serves as the site of cellular respiration (electron transport chain), since prokaryotes lack mitochondria.
callouts.ap_tip: Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes have plasma membranes — this is a SHARED feature. However, in prokaryotes the plasma membrane performs functions (like the ETC) that are handled by mitochondrial membranes in eukaryotes. The AP exam frequently asks what structures are shared between prokaryotes and eukaryotes.

callouts.label: Nucleoid region
callouts.hint: Irregular teal-colored zone in the center of the prokaryotic cell containing tangled DNA — no membrane around it.
callouts.description: The nucleoid is the region of the prokaryotic cell where the circular chromosome is concentrated. Unlike a eukaryotic nucleus, the nucleoid is NOT enclosed by a membrane — the DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm. The prokaryotic chromosome is a single, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule that is supercoiled to fit within the cell.
callouts.ap_tip: The key distinction: prokaryotes have a nucleoid (no membrane), eukaryotes have a nucleus (double membrane with pores). This is the defining difference between the two domains. The AP exam tests this repeatedly — 'membrane-bound nucleus' is the hallmark of eukaryotes.

callouts.label: Plasmid
callouts.hint: Small separate teal ring of DNA near the edge of the nucleoid in the prokaryotic cell.
callouts.description: Plasmids are small, circular, extrachromosomal DNA molecules found in many prokaryotes. They replicate independently of the main chromosome and often carry genes for antibiotic resistance, toxin production, or metabolic capabilities. Plasmids can be transferred between bacteria via conjugation (horizontal gene transfer).
callouts.ap_tip: Plasmids are central to biotechnology — they are used as vectors in genetic engineering to insert foreign genes into bacteria. The AP exam tests plasmid-based cloning and transformation. Plasmids also explain how antibiotic resistance spreads rapidly through bacterial populations via horizontal gene transfer.

callouts.label: Ribosomes (70S)
callouts.hint: Many tiny dark gray dots scattered throughout the prokaryotic cytoplasm — smaller than eukaryotic ribosomes.
callouts.description: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S particles (composed of 30S and 50S subunits), smaller than eukaryotic 80S ribosomes. Despite the size difference, they perform the same essential function: translating mRNA into polypeptide chains. Both types of ribosomes are present in all living cells, reflecting their ancient evolutionary origin.
callouts.ap_tip: Ribosome size difference (70S vs. 80S) is exploited by antibiotics — drugs like tetracycline and streptomycin target 70S ribosomes specifically, killing bacteria without harming human cells. Note: mitochondria and chloroplasts also have 70S ribosomes, supporting endosymbiotic theory.

callouts.label: Flagellum
callouts.hint: Long wavy dark green whip-like structure extending from one end of the prokaryotic cell.
callouts.description: The bacterial flagellum is a rotating, whip-like appendage used for motility. It is powered by a proton motive force (not ATP directly) through a molecular motor embedded in the cell wall and membrane. Bacterial flagella are structurally different from eukaryotic flagella — they are made of the protein flagellin and rotate like a propeller, whereas eukaryotic flagella use a 9+2 microtubule arrangement and wave back and forth.
callouts.ap_tip: Prokaryotic and eukaryotic flagella are NOT homologous — they evolved independently (analogous structures). Bacterial flagella rotate; eukaryotic flagella undulate. The AP exam may ask you to compare their structure and mechanism. This is also an example of convergent evolution at the cellular level.

callouts.label: Pili
callouts.hint: Short olive-green stubby projections on the outer surface of the prokaryotic cell, much shorter than the flagellum.
callouts.description: Pili (singular: pilus) are short, hair-like protein appendages on the surface of many bacteria. They serve two main functions: attachment pili help bacteria adhere to surfaces and host cells (important for pathogenicity), while sex pili (F pili) form a bridge between two bacterial cells during conjugation, enabling horizontal gene transfer of plasmid DNA.
callouts.ap_tip: Conjugation via sex pili is a form of horizontal gene transfer — distinct from vertical inheritance (parent to offspring). The AP exam tests the three modes of horizontal gene transfer in prokaryotes: conjugation (pili), transformation (uptake of free DNA), and transduction (via bacteriophages).

callouts.label: Nucleus
callouts.hint: Large dark blue oval with a double-membrane envelope in the upper portion of the eukaryotic cell on the right.
callouts.description: The nucleus is the defining feature of eukaryotic cells. It is enclosed by a double-membrane nuclear envelope with nuclear pores that regulate transport of mRNA, ribosomal subunits, and proteins between the nucleus and cytoplasm. Inside, DNA is organized into linear chromosomes associated with histone proteins (chromatin). The nucleus is the site of DNA replication and transcription.
callouts.ap_tip: The presence of a membrane-bound nucleus is THE defining feature of eukaryotes (eu = true, karyon = kernel/nucleus). The nuclear envelope is continuous with the rough ER. Nuclear pores are selectively permeable — they allow mRNA export and protein import but keep chromosomes inside.

callouts.label: Nucleolus
callouts.hint: Small dense purple circle inside the nucleus of the eukaryotic cell.
callouts.description: The nucleolus is a dense, non-membrane-bound region within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is transcribed and assembled with ribosomal proteins into ribosomal subunits. These subunits are then exported through nuclear pores to the cytoplasm, where they combine to form functional ribosomes. Cells with high protein synthesis demands (like secretory cells) often have prominent nucleoli.
callouts.ap_tip: The nucleolus is NOT a separate organelle — it has no membrane. It is simply a region of the nucleus specialized for rRNA production. Do not confuse it with the nucleus itself. The AP exam occasionally includes the nucleolus as a distractor in questions about membrane-bound organelles.

callouts.label: Rough ER
callouts.hint: Network of blue-purple folded membranes with ribosome dots on the surface, extending from the nuclear envelope in the eukaryotic cell.
callouts.description: The rough ER is a network of membrane-enclosed sacs (cisternae) studded with ribosomes on its outer surface. It is continuous with the nuclear envelope. Ribosomes on the rough ER synthesize proteins destined for secretion, membrane insertion, or delivery to lysosomes. These proteins enter the ER lumen as they are translated, where they are folded and processed (including glycosylation).
callouts.ap_tip: The rough ER is part of the endomembrane system: rough ER → transport vesicles → Golgi apparatus → secretory vesicles → plasma membrane. Know this pathway in order — it is a favorite AP Biology FRQ topic. Proteins made on FREE ribosomes stay in the cytoplasm; proteins made on ROUGH ER ribosomes are exported.

callouts.label: Golgi apparatus
callouts.hint: Stacked gold/amber curved cisternae with small vesicles budding from the ends, in the eukaryotic cell.
callouts.description: The Golgi apparatus is a stack of flattened membrane cisternae that receives, modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids arriving from the ER in transport vesicles. Modifications include further glycosylation, phosphorylation, and proteolytic cleavage. The Golgi has a cis face (receiving side, near ER) and a trans face (shipping side, facing plasma membrane), with vesicles budding from the trans face for delivery to their final destinations.
callouts.ap_tip: Think of the Golgi as the cell's 'post office' — it receives packages (proteins), adds address labels (sugar tags), and ships them to the correct destination. The AP exam tests the cis-to-trans directionality and the Golgi's role in the secretory pathway.

callouts.label: Mitochondria
callouts.hint: Red-orange oval organelles with visible internal folds (cristae) in the eukaryotic cell.
callouts.description: Mitochondria are the site of aerobic cellular respiration, where glucose is fully oxidized to CO₂ and H₂O, producing ~30-32 ATP per glucose via the Krebs cycle (in the matrix) and the electron transport chain (on the inner membrane cristae). Mitochondria have a double membrane, their own circular DNA, and 70S ribosomes — evidence for their endosymbiotic origin from ancient aerobic bacteria.
callouts.ap_tip: Mitochondria are a key piece of evidence for endosymbiotic theory: they have their own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes (like bacteria), double membranes, and reproduce by binary fission. The AP exam tests endosymbiotic theory and expects you to cite these four lines of evidence for both mitochondria and chloroplasts.

callouts.label: Ribosomes (80S)
callouts.hint: Dark gray dots in the eukaryotic cytoplasm and on the rough ER surface — slightly larger than prokaryotic ribosomes.
callouts.description: Eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S particles (composed of 40S and 60S subunits), larger than prokaryotic 70S ribosomes. Free ribosomes in the cytoplasm synthesize proteins for intracellular use (cytoplasmic enzymes, structural proteins). Ribosomes bound to the rough ER synthesize proteins for export, membrane insertion, or lysosomal targeting.
callouts.ap_tip: Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes have ribosomes — this is a SHARED feature and reflects the universal need for protein synthesis. However, the size difference (70S vs. 80S) is clinically important: antibiotics that target 70S ribosomes kill bacteria but not human cells. This selective toxicity is a common AP Biology exam topic.

callouts.label: Plasma membrane
callouts.hint: Thin blue outer boundary of the eukaryotic animal cell on the right — no cell wall outside it.
callouts.description: The eukaryotic plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded and peripheral proteins, cholesterol (in animal cells), and glycoproteins. It serves the same basic functions as the prokaryotic membrane — selective permeability and cell signaling — but does NOT house the electron transport chain (that function is in the mitochondrial inner membrane in eukaryotes).
callouts.ap_tip: Animal cells have a plasma membrane but NO cell wall (unlike plant cells, fungi, and most prokaryotes). This makes animal cells more flexible but also susceptible to osmotic lysis in hypotonic solutions. The AP exam tests the fluid mosaic model — know that the membrane is dynamic, with proteins floating in the lipid bilayer.

callouts.label: Lysosomes
callouts.hint: Small brown-orange spheres scattered in the eukaryotic cytoplasm.
callouts.description: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles containing hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes that break down macromolecules, damaged organelles, and engulfed pathogens. They maintain an acidic internal pH (~4.5-5.0) optimal for their enzymes. Lysosomes are produced by the Golgi apparatus and are unique to animal cells — plant cells use the central vacuole for similar degradative functions.
callouts.ap_tip: Lysosomes are part of the endomembrane system (produced by the Golgi). Their low internal pH is maintained by proton pumps in the lysosomal membrane. Tay-Sachs disease results from a missing lysosomal enzyme — this is a classic AP Biology example of how organelle dysfunction causes disease.
